trajectory.description: Observation: Handler returned df with 159 columns total but no 'feature' multi-level. The shape (914654, 159) suggests label is included as one column; columns are flat. Need to check column structure. With data_key='learn', features+labels are returned but column structure depends. Let me handle both flat and multi-index. Actually with fetch, it should return MultiIndex columns. Let me just split: last col is label, rest are features. Or use handler.fetch with col_set. Decision: use col_set='feature' and col_set='label' separately.

trajectory.description: Observation: Training succeeded but stopped at only 24 iterations - very early. valid l2=0.995 means little signal extracted. Analysis: Need to verify pipeline works end-to-end first. Let me submit eval to get baseline, measure time. Decision: submit eval now.

Observation: Eval returned 0 for everything with total_days=0. The predict.py is finding zero days. Analysis: Two likely issues: (1) Alpha158 with end_time=test_period[1]=2023-12-31 might not be loadable if data ends earlier; (2) more likely the label DataFrame has MultiIndex (instrument, datetime) but groupby(level=0) groups by instrument not date. In Qlib, the convention is index level 0 = datetime, level 1 = instrument. Need to verify. Also, the per_day groupby — if level 0 is instrument, that's wrong. Let me check: handler.fetch returns DataFrame with index (datetime, instrument) typically. Then groupby(level=0) = groupby datetime which is correct. But total_days=0 suggests df is empty after concat. Most likely Alpha158 init with end_time=2023-12-31 but the handler's fit_end is 2020 so test data is in 'infer' but normalization references training period. Let me debug locally.

Decision: Add debug print to predict.py to see what's happening, and run it locally to debug.

Observation: feat index is (datetime, instrument), label index is (instrument, datetime) — different ordering. concat axis=1 fails to align properly because of different index level order, dropping all rows. Decision: swap label index levels to match, or reset and re-index. Fix predict.py and re-evaluate.

trajectory.description: Observation: Baseline IC=0.0435 confirmed. Pipeline solid. Analysis: LightGBM with current params only ran ~24 iters before early stopping — likely too aggressive learning rate or noisy validation. The Qlib official LightGBM recipe for Alpha158 CSI300 typically achieves IC~0.05+. Let me improve: (1) lower learning rate, more rounds; (2) add label normalization (CSRankNorm or CSZScoreNorm) which often helps; (3) try ensembling multiple seeds. Decision: Retrain with better hyperparameters using Alpha158 with proper label processors. Use the standard Alpha158 with CSZScoreNorm label normalization. Then ensemble multiple seeds.

trajectory.description: Observation: Training stopped very early (~50-60 rounds) with valid l2=0.996 — essentially flat. This is likely because CSRankNorm scales labels to a tight rank-based range with very small variance, so MSE doesn't decrease much numerically but model is still learning. However the early stopping tolerance is too tight. Models trained, predict.py is solid (IC 0.0435 baseline). Analysis: I should evaluate this 3-seed ensemble first before more changes — early stopping fired late enough that meaningful learning happened. Let me submit eval to see if ensembling helps. Decision: submit eval.

trajectory.description:
Observation: 3-seed ensemble with CSRankNorm gave IC=0.0462, RankIC=0.0565 — improvement. Training stopped at ~50-60 rounds though, very early. The CSRankNorm makes labels uniform on rank scale, so MSE is naturally near 1.0 and small numerical changes are meaningful but early stopping with patience 80 and log_evaluation 200 means it stopped at ~140-150. Best iter 50 means valid l2 didn't improve after iter 50. 

Analysis: To boost IC further, options: (1) use Huber loss (more robust); (2) smaller learning rate with more patience; (3) try without CSRankNorm on labels (use raw or CSZScoreNorm) — CSRankNorm sometimes hurts since it removes variance info; (4) ensemble more seeds; (5) use DropExtremeLabel. Let me try CSZScoreNorm (standard Qlib lgb_csi300 config) which usually scores higher on raw IC. Also increase seeds to 5, lower lr to 0.01 for more iterations.

Decision: Modify train.py to use CSZScoreNorm label, lower lr, more patience, 5 seeds. Submit eval.

Observation: 5-seed ensemble training done with CSZScoreNorm label, lr=0.01, but my predict.py still uses CSRankNorm in learn_processors. That doesn't matter since predict only uses 'infer' col_set='feature' — labels processors don't affect inference features. Good. Need to submit eval to see if CSZScoreNorm + 5 seeds + lr 0.01 improves.

Decision: Submit eval.

trajectory.description: Observation: Best so far is 0.0462 (CSRankNorm + 3 seeds). Recent CSZScoreNorm + 5 seeds gave 0.0447 — worse. CSRankNorm helps RankIC more. Analysis: To improve further: (1) revert to CSRankNorm; (2) try DropExtremeLabel + CSRankNorm; (3) ensemble more seeds. Also could try combining the CSRankNorm 3-seed predictions with CSZScoreNorm 5-seed — meta-ensemble. Let me retrain with CSRankNorm + DropExtremeLabel + more seeds (8) + slightly tweaked params for diversity. Decision: retrain with CSRankNorm, 7 seeds, lower lr=0.02, num_leaves variation across seeds for diversity.

trajectory.description: Observation: Best is still iter 8 (0.0462) with 3-seed CSRankNorm. The 7-seed diverse params actually did worse on IC. Analysis: Diversification with extreme params hurt. The simple 3-seed CSRankNorm config worked best. Let me restore that exact recipe but add tweaks: try Huber loss or just retrain with the original 3-seed config + maybe ensemble across CSRankNorm and CSZScoreNorm models to combine their strengths. Actually best approach: revert to the proven 3-seed CSRankNorm config and try larger ensemble of similar (not divergent) seeds. Also try adding feature engineering or try MSE on raw labels with z-score per cross-section. Decision: Keep things simple - revert to 3-seed CSRankNorm with default params (which scored 0.0462). Use 5 seeds with same params, only varying random_state to keep diversity through bootstrap not parameter changes.

trajectory.description: Observation: Just achieved new best IC=0.04689 with the 3-seed CSRankNorm config (slightly better than iter-8's 0.0462). icir=0.3365, rank_ic=0.0553. Analysis: I have 14 iters and 3.47h left. The simple config beats more complex variants. Let me try one more variation: ensemble this 3-seed model with a slightly different config (e.g., add 2 more seeds with different num_leaves) by averaging predictions. Or try with more careful tuning. Actually safest path: try ensembling with linear model (Ridge on Alpha158) which is known to give good IC and has different errors than GBDT. Actually, let me try simple: increase to 5 seeds with same exact config to see if more seeds of same config helps. Decision: Try 5 seeds same config to see if averaging more reduces variance. Keep current best safe.
